35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
79	    thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
80	
81	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
82	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
83	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
84	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
85	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
86	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
87	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
88	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
89	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
90	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
91	  %  [\dots]
92	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
93	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
94	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
95	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
96	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
97	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
98	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
99	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
100	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
101	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
102	  \[
103	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
104	  \]
105	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
106	  \[
107	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
108	  \]
109	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
110	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
111	  \[
112	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
113	  \]
114	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
115	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
116	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
117	\end{lemma}
118

143	\begin{proof}
144	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
145	    thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
146	    lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
147	    lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
148	    lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup, lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
149	    lem:pullback_unit_iso, lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv}
150	
151	
152	\leanok
153	  The construction proceeds in four steps over the locally-trivial carrier
154	  \[
155	    \mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T) \;=\;
156	      \bigl\{\, M : \text{a module on } C \times_k T \;\bigm|\;
157	      M \text{ is locally trivial} \,\bigr\}
158	  \]
159	  of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, on whose isomorphism classes the
160	  relative Picard quotient is taken. It is authored \emph{in parallel} against
161	  two inputs being built concurrently: the locally-trivial pullback--tensor
162	  comparison isomorphism \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} (consumed in
163	  Step~2 to make \(\pi_T^*\) additive) and the tensor inverse
164	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (consumed in Step~1 for group
165	  closure); both are themselves deferred targets.
166	
167	  \emph{Step 1 (abelian group on the carrier).} On isomorphism classes of the
168	  carrier \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T)\), addition is the descent of the
169	  scheme-level tensor product of line bundles,
170	  \[
171	    [L] + [L'] \;:=\; [\, L \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} L' \,],
172	  \]
173	  where \(L \otimes L'\) is the locally-trivial descent of the scheme-level
174	  tensor product (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}); the sum lands again in
175	  the carrier because the tensor product of two locally-trivial modules is
176	  locally trivial (\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}). The zero
177	  element is the class \([\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\) of the structure sheaf,
178	  the monoidal unit, which is locally trivial. Associativity, commutativity,
179	  and left/right unitality of \(+\) descend from the scheme-level coherence
180	  isomorphisms --- the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the
181	  braiding (\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}), and the unitors
182	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) --- exactly as for the absolute Picard group
183	  on tensor-invertible isomorphism classes
184	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The additive inverse is
185	  \[
186	    -[L] \;:=\; [\, L^{\mathrm{inv}} \,],
187	  \]
188	  where \(L^{\mathrm{inv}}\) is the witness supplied by
189	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}: it returns a \emph{locally-trivial}
190	  module together with an isomorphism
191	  \(L \otimes L^{\mathrm{inv}} \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\), so the inverse
192	  stays inside the loc-triv carrier and the group is closed under negation.
193	
194	  \emph{Step 2 (the pullback homomorphism \(\pi_T^*\)).} Pullback along the
195	  projection \(\pi_T\) gives an additive map
196	  \[
197	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T),
198	  \]
199	  whose underlying assignment of line bundles is
200	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}. It is a homomorphism of abelian groups:
201	  it sends the unit to the unit, since
202	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{O}_T \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) by the unconditional
203	  pullback--unit isomorphism (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}); and it is additive,
204	  since pullback commutes with tensor product on locally-trivial modules,
205	  \[
206	    \pi_T^*(N \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} N') \;\cong\;
207	      \pi_T^* N \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^* N',
208	  \]
209	  by the locally-trivial comparison isomorphism
210	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} specialised to the projection
211	  \(\pi_T\) on the \(\mathrm{OnProduct}\) carrier. This is the exact analogue,
212	  for the geometric pullback, of the additive monoid homomorphism on Picard
213	  groups induced by a ring map respecting tensor product, and is modelled on it
214	  field-for-field. Write \(H_T := \mathrm{im}(\pi_T^*)\) for its image, a
215	  subgroup of the carrier's isomorphism-class group.
216	
217	  \emph{Step 3 (setoid reconciliation).} The set-valued quotient of
218	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is taken modulo the
219	  isomorphism-class equivalence that declares two line bundles related when
220	  their classes differ by an element of \(H_T\). This relation coincides with
221	  the left-coset relation of the subgroup \(H_T\) inside the abelian group of
222	  Steps~1--2: both encode
223	  \[
224	    L \sim L' \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
225	      [\, L^{\mathrm{inv}} \otimes L' \,] \in H_T .
226	  \]
227	  Establishing this equivalence of relations is a concrete isomorphism-class
228	  argument over the carrier; it consumes the tensor inverse (Step~1) and the
229	  group law, but not the computational content of the comparison isomorphism.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 4 (transport).} By Step~3 the quotient set underlying
232	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is in canonical bijection with the group quotient
233	  \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T) / H_T\), which carries the canonical
234	  abelian-group structure of a quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup.
235	  Transporting this structure along the bijection of Step~3 endows
236	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) with its abelian-group structure, under which the
237	  canonical surjection
238	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a
239	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
240	\end{proof}
241	
242	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
243	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
244	
245	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
246	group-valued presheaf.

427	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
428	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
429	
430	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
431	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
432	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
433	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
434	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
435	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
436	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
437	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
438	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.

565	  separately as \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical} below.
566	\end{theorem}
567

598	  The canonical morphism from a presheaf to its associated sheaf in
599	  any Grothendieck topology (Stacks tag 00WI; the canonical

610	\begin{theorem}[Canonical sheafification unit with universal property (forward-looking)]
611	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
612	
613	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
614	  % Def.~df:Pfs + the associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following
615	  % (the \'etale sheaf is characterised as the sheaf associated to the
616	  % relative Picard functor, via the natural map from a presheaf to its
617	  % associated sheaf --- i.e.\ the sheafification unit with its universal
618	  % property)
619	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1328).
620	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
621	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
622	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
623	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
624	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
625	  % topologies  by
626	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
627	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
628	  %  \end{dfn}
629	  %  [\dots]
630	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
631	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
632	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
633	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
634	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
635	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
636	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
637	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
638	  Def.~df:Pfs together with the associated-sheaf paragraph
639	  immediately following (the natural map from a presheaf to its
640	  associated sheaf, taken inside the category of abelian groups, is
641	  universal among presheaf morphisms from \(\Pic_{X/S}\) into \'etale
642	  sheaves of abelian groups).}
643	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. There
644	  exists a canonical morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
645	  \[
646	    \eta \;\colon\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\;
647	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}
648	  \]
649	  with the following universal property. For every \'etale sheaf
650	  \(\mathcal{F}\) of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and every morphism
651	  of presheaves of abelian groups
652	  \(\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F}\), there is a unique
653	  morphism of \'etale sheaves of abelian groups
654	  \(\tilde\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \mathcal{F}\) such that
655	  \(\tilde\varphi \circ \eta = \varphi\). Equivalently, the pair
656	  \(\bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}, \, \eta\bigr)\) is initial among
657	  pairs \((\mathcal{F}, \, \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F})\)
658	  consisting of an \'etale sheaf of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and
659	  a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)
660	  to \(\mathcal{F}\). In particular, the abelian-group structure on
661	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) at each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) is uniquely
662	  determined (up to canonical isomorphism) by the abelian-group
663	  structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) and the universal property of
664	  \(\eta\).
665	\end{theorem}
666	
667	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs itself, Kleiman \S 2 does not
668	% supply a separate proof body --- the universal property is built
669	% into ``the sheaf associated to'' as a synonym for sheafification,
670	% as the verbatim quote above already makes plain. The project-side
671	% argument is the standard universal property of sheafification in a
672	% Grothendieck topology, transported through the colimit-preservation
673	% of \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\).
674	\begin{proof}
675	
676	  In any Grothendieck topology, every presheaf has a canonical
677	  associated sheaf, and the canonical morphism from a presheaf to its
678	  associated sheaf is universal among morphisms from the presheaf into
679	  sheaves (Stacks tag 00WI; Kleiman \S 2, paragraph following
680	  df:Pfs). The \'etale site of \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck
681	  topology, so this applies in particular to \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) and
682	  yields the canonical set-valued sheafification
683	  \((\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) together with the universal arrow
684	  out of \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The forgetful functor
685	  \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves and reflects the
686	  filtered colimits and finite limits used by the plus-construction,
687	  so performing the same plus-construction inside \(\AddCommGroup\)
688	  yields a sheaf of abelian groups whose underlying set-valued sheaf
689	  coincides with the set-valued sheafification; the abelian-group
690	  structure on the sheafification is transported back through this
691	  identification. The resulting morphism \(\eta\) is then by
692	  construction the universal arrow in the abelian-group-valued
693	  sheafification, which is the universal property in the form stated.
694	\end{proof}

301	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
302	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
303	
304	\begin{definition}
305	
306	
307	\leanok
308	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
309	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
311	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
312	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
313	  \[
314	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
315	  \]
316	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
317	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
318	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
319	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
320	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
321	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
322	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
323	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
324	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
325	  canonical compatibility
326	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
327	     \;\cong\;
328	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
329	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
330	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
331	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
332	
333	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
334	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
335	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
336	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
337	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
338	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
339	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
340	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{lemma}
344	
345	
346	\leanok
347	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
348	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
350	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
351	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
352	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
353	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
354	  \[
355	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
357	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
358	  \]
359	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
360	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
361	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
362	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
363	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
364	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
365	  \[
366	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
367	    \qquad
368	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
369	  \]
370	  the associator
371	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
372	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
373	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
374	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
375	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
376	\end{lemma}
377	
378	\begin{proof}
379	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
380	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
381	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
382	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
383	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
384	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
385	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
386	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
387	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
388	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
389	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
390	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
391	\end{proof}
392	
393	\begin{remark}
394	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
395	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
396	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
397	        lem:whisker_of_W}
398	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
399	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
400	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
401	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
402	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
403	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
404	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
405	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
406	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
407	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
408	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
409	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
410	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
411	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
412	
413	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
414	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
415	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
416	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
417	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
418	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
419	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
420	  fixed-base presheaves in
421	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
422	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
423	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
424	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
425	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
426	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
427	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
428	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
429	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
430	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
431	
432	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
433	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
434	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
435	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
436	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
437	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
438	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
439	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
440	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
441	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
442	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
443	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
444	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
445	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
446	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
447	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
448	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
449	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
450	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
451	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
452	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
453	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
454	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
455	\end{remark}
456	
457	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
458	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
459	
460	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
461	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
462	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
463	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
464	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
465	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
466	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
467	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
468	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
469	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
470	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
471	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
472	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
473	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
474	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
475	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
476	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
477	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
478	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
479	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
480	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
481	to the off-path route~(e).
482	
483	\begin{lemma}
484	
485	\leanok
486	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
487	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
488	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
489	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
490	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
491	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
492	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
493	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
494	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
495	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
496	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
497	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
498	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
499	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
500	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
501	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
502	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
503	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
504	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
505	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
506	  \[
507	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
508	      \;\;:=\;\;
509	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
510	  \]
511	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
512	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
513	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
514	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
515	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
516	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
517	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
518	\end{lemma}
519	
520	\begin{proof}
521	
522	
523	\leanok
524	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
525	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
526	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
527	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
528	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
529	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
530	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
531	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
532	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
533	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
534	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
535	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
536	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
537	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
538	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
539	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
540	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
541	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
542	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
543	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a definitional
544	  transport across the unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
545	
546	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
547	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
548	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
549	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
550	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
551	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
552	\end{proof}
553	
554	\begin{lemma}
555	
556	
557	\leanok
558	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
559	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
560	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
561	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
562	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
563	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
564	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
565	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
566	  morphism
567	  \[
568	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
569	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
570	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
571	  \]
572	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
573	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
574	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
575	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
576	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
577	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
578	\end{lemma}
579	
580	\begin{proof}
581	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
582	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
583	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
584	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
585	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
586	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
587	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
588	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
589	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
590	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
591	  isomorphism}
592	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
593	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
594	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
595	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
596	  therefore extension of scalars along
597	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
598	
599	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction

2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}

15	% (Quot / Hilbert representability), A.3 (identity component), and A.4
16	% (universal property = Albanese / Jacobian).
17	
18	% NOTE (planner directive iter-173). Per the iter-173 blueprint-review
19	% recommendation (see .archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-route173.md,
20	% sub-phase choice), this chapter defines \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a
21	% \emph{set-valued} presheaf on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). The abelian-group refinement
22	% (and the \'etale-sheafification step) belong to the follow-up chapter
23	% Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex (A.1.c). The set-valued formulation lets us discharge
24	% well-definedness of the quotient and functoriality of \(g^*\) as soon as a
25	% line-bundle pullback functor and its composition identity are in hand, with

27	% does not yet exist.
28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an
78	  open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(C \times_k T\) on which each restriction
79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}

127	\section{Project-side IsLocallyTrivial substrate}
128	\label{sec:lbp_isLocallyTrivial_substrate}
129	
130	Mathlib at the pinned commit \texttt{b80f227} ships no
131	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible} predicate (iter-187 \texttt{lean\_local\_search}
132	verdict: empty). The project introduces a stand-in predicate
133	\texttt{Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial} encoding rank-one local
134	trivialisation on an affine cover. The subtype
135	\texttt{\{ M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M \}}
136	gives the carrier of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}.
137	
138	\begin{definition}
139	
140	\leanok
141	[Local triviality of a \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
142	  \label{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}
144	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
145	  (locally trivial $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules), specialised to rank one.}
146	  An \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\) on a scheme \(X\) is
147	  \emph{locally trivial of rank one} if for every point \(x \in X\) there
148	  exists an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an iso
149	  \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (the structure sheaf restricted to
150	  \(U\) viewed as the unit object of \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\)). Formally:
151	  \[
152	    \forall\, x : X, \exists\, U : X.\mathrm{Opens},
153	      x \in U \,\wedge\, \mathtt{IsAffineOpen}\, U \,\wedge\,
154	      \mathtt{Nonempty}\, (\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{restrict}\, U.\iota \cong
155	        \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\, \_).
156	  \]
157	\end{definition}
158	
159	\begin{lemma}
160	
161	\leanok
162	[Pullback preserves local triviality (Stacks 01HH)]
163	  \label{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}
164	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}
165	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
166	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
167	  (pullback of a locally trivial module is locally trivial).}
168	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
169	  \(\mathcal{M} : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be locally trivial of rank one
170	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}). Then \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is locally
171	  trivial of rank one.
172	\end{lemma}
173	\begin{proof}
174	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
175	  \leanok
176	  Given \(y : Y\), let \(x = f(y) \in X\). By the local triviality of
177	  \(\mathcal{M}\) at \(x\), there is an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an
178	  iso \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The preimage \(f^{-1}U\)
179	  is an open neighbourhood of \(y\), and any sufficiently small affine
180	  open \(V \subseteq f^{-1}U\) containing \(y\) (exists by
181	  \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}) gives the chart. The
182	  required iso
183	  \((f^*\mathcal{M})|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) follows from the chain:
184	  \texttt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback} (identifying \(\mathrm{restrict}\, V.\iota\)
185	  with \(\mathrm{pullback}\, V.\iota\)), \texttt{pullbackComp} (composing
186	  pullback functors along \(V.\iota \circ f = (g : V \to U) \circ U.\iota\)),
187	  and \texttt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit} (pullback of unit is unit; uses
188	  \(\mathtt{F.Final}\) instance for the underlying continuous functor of an
189	  open immersion).
190	
191	  % NOTE (iter-188 plan-phase): Body lands with 1 narrow named typed sorry
192	  % on the residual chart-iso construction; the affine-chart existence step
193	  % is closed axiom-clean. Closure target iter-188 prover, ~30-50 LOC.
194	\end{proof}

196	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
197	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
198	
199	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
200	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
201	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
202	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
203	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
204	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
205	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.
206	
207	\begin{definition}\leanok
208	[Pullback along the projection]
209	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
213	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
214	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
216	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
217	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
218	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
219	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
220	  %  [\dots]
221	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
222	  % sense: given any family of  \(S\)-schemes \(T_i\), we have
223	  %     \(\)\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).\(\)"
224	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
225	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
226	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
227	  Let \(C/k\) and \(T/k\) be as in
228	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\)
229	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along \(\pi_T\)} is the
230	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
231	  in this chapter)
232	  \[
233	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
234	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
235	  \]
236	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module
237	  \(\mathcal{N}\) to the isomorphism class of \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}\), an invertible
238	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
239	  defined because pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules respects isomorphism, and is
240	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
241	  \(\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C\) that we quotient by in the definition of
242	  the relative Picard presheaf.
243	\end{definition}
244	
245	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as
246	% the direct application `(Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N`,
247	% axiom-clean modulo the carrier-level simplification on `OnProduct`. The
248	% invertibility-preservation step (Stacks 01HH: pullback of invertible is
249	% invertible) is deferred to iter-187+ once `OnProduct` carries an
250	% invertibility witness.
251	
252	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
253	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
254	
255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
256	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
257	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
259	  
260	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
261	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
262	  % includes composition)
263	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
265	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
266	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
267	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
268	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
269	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
270	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
271	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
272	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
273	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
274	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
275	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
276	  canonical projections, and write
277	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
278	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
279	  pullback maps
280	  \[
281	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
282	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
283	  \]
284	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
285	  \[
286	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
287	  \]
288	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
289	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
290	\end{lemma}
291	
292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
293	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
294	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
295	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
296	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
297	\begin{proof}
298	  
299	  
300	\leanok
301	  The square
302	  \[
303	    \begin{array}{ccc}
304	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
305	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
306	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
307	    \end{array}
308	  \]
309	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
310	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
311	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
312	  there is a canonical isomorphism
313	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
314	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
315	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
316	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
317	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
318	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
319	  set-valued maps stated above.
320	\end{proof}
321	
322	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
323	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
324	
325	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
326	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
327	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
328	
329	\begin{theorem}\leanok
330	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
331	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
335	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
336	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
338	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
339	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
340	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
341	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
342	  % topologies  by
343	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
344	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
345	  %  \end{dfn}"
346	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
347	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
348	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
349	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
350	  \[
351	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
352	  \]
353	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
354	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
355	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
356	  a subgroup). Define
357	  \[
358	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
359	  \]
360	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
361	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
362	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
363	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
364	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
365	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
366	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as a
370	% `Setoid` on `OnProduct = (pullback πC πT).Modules` whose relation is
371	% `r L L' := Nonempty (L ≅ L')` (iso-class equivalence; axiom-clean). This
372	% identifies isomorphic modules (giving the underlying set of `Pic(C ×_S T)`
373	% once invertibility is imposed in iter-187+) but does NOT yet quotient by
374	% the pullback subgroup `pi_T^* Pic(T)`. The full quotient requires (a) a
375	% tensor-product structure on `Scheme.Modules` (Mathlib ships
376	% `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` but no monoidal structure on
377	% `Scheme.Modules` directly at b80f227), and (b) an inverse for invertible
378	% sheaves (depends on the iter-187+ `IsInvertible` predicate). Iter-187+
379	% refinement: `r L L' := exists N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L tensor L'inv iso
380	% pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available.
381	
382	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
383	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
384	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
385	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
386	% not a translation of an external argument.
387	\begin{proof}
388	  
389	  
390	\leanok
391	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
392	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
393	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
394	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
395	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
396	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
397	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
398	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is the canonical quotient map
399	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
400	\end{proof}
401	
402	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
403	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
404	
405	\begin{theorem}\leanok
406	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
407	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
408	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
409	  
410	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
411	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
412	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
415	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
416	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
417	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
418	  %  [\dots]
419	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
420	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
421	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
422	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
423	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
424	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
425	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
426	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
427	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
428	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
429	  \[
430	    \begin{array}{ccc}
431	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
432	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
433	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
434	    \end{array}
435	  \]
436	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
437	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
438	  unique set map
439	  \[
440	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
441	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
442	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
443	  \]
444	  through the quotients of
445	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
446	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
447	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
448	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
449	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
450	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
451	\end{theorem}
452	
453	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
454	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
455	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
456	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
457	\begin{proof}
458	  
459	  
460	\leanok
461	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
462	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
463	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
464	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
465	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
466	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
467	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
468	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
469	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
470	  universal property of the quotient set
471	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
472	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
473	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
474	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
475	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
476	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
477	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
478	\end{proof}

480	\section{Lean encoding}
481	\label{sec:lbp_lean_encoding}
482	
483	The Lean target file is the new
484	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}, sibling to
485	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
486	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
487	Lean targets as follows.
488	
489	\begin{itemize}
490	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
491	    (\cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}) is a re-export / type alias for the
492	    Mathlib invertible-module predicate on \(C \times_k T\). Mathlib provides
493	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Invertible} (or the equivalent
494	    bundle-of-rank-one packaging in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle});
495	    the project-side declaration simply specialises the Mathlib predicate to
496	    the product scheme.
497	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
498	    (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}) is the pullback map on Picard
499	    groups induced by \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\). The Mathlib backbone is
500	    the pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
501	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullback}); the project-side
502	    declaration restricts that pullback to invertible modules and proves
503	    invertibility is preserved (Stacks tag 01HH; one open-cover unfolding).
504	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback\_pullback\_eq}
505	    (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}) is the equality of pullback maps on
506	    isomorphism classes; the Lean form is the propositional equality of two
507	    \texttt{LinearMap}s (or \texttt{Quotient.mk}s) deduced from Mathlib's
508	    pullback-of-modules transitivity.
509	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
510	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}) is the
511	    structural existence of the quotient set
512	    \(\Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\). Mathlib's quotient-set machinery
513	    (\texttt{Quotient}, \texttt{QuotientGroup}) suffices; the project-side
514	    declaration only packages the universal property in the form
515	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} expects.
516	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
517	    (\cref{thm:pullback_natural}) is the functor instance
518	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\). The naturality square
519	    of \cref{lem:pullback_compose} is the data; the identity and composition
520	    laws are the lemmas \texttt{id\_eq} and \texttt{comp\_eq} of the
521	    quotient-induced map.
522	\end{itemize}
523	
524	No new core axiom is required. The chapter is a definitional / structural
525	construction on top of the existing Mathlib invertible-module + pullback
526	infrastructure, plus the project's relative-spectrum chapter
527	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) for any concrete Lean unfolding that the
528	prover wishes to perform on an affine open of \(C \times_k T\).
