3937	
3938	  \emph{Sq2 (the \(\delta\) core).} The presheaf-level oplax comparison \(\delta\) of the
3939	  \emph{composite} pullback decomposes by the Mathlib oplax-monoidal coherence
3940	  \(\mathtt{Functor.OplaxMonoidal.comp\_}\delta\), once the composite presheaf pullback
3941	  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{h \circ f}\) is identified with the composite functor
3942	  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{f} \,\circ\, \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{h}\) through the
3943	  Mathlib presheaf coherence \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackComp}\). This
3944	  identification rests on the ring-map reconciliation
3945	  \[
3946	    (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,(h \circ f)).\mathrm{hom}
3947	      \;=\; \varphi'_{f} \;\mathbin{;}\;
3948	      (\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base})^{\mathrm{op}}.\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\,\varphi'_{h},
3949	  \]
3950	  which is \emph{definitional}: although the two sides are presented in functor categories
3951	  that nominally differ by the equality \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,(h.\mathrm{base} \circ
3952	  f.\mathrm{base}) = \mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base} \,\circ\, \mathtt{Opens.map}\,
3953	  h.\mathrm{base}\) (\(\mathtt{Opens.map\_comp}\)), this equality and the underlying
3954	  scheme-composition data already hold up to definitional equality at default
3955	  transparency, so the reconciliation is definitional
3956	  (\(\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom\_comp\_hom\_reconcile}\)). No pseudofunctor-coherence
3957	  transport and none of the associativity/unit bookkeeping atoms
3958	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.}\{\mathtt{pullbackId}, \mathtt{pullback\_assoc},
3959	  \mathtt{pullback\_comp\_id}, \mathtt{pullback\_id\_comp}\}\) are required for this
3960	  reconciliation. The genuine content of Sq2 is therefore not the reconciliation but the
3961	  monoidality of \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) itself, isolated as Sq2b below.
3962	
3963	  \emph{Sq2b (monoidality of \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) --- the genuinely new ingredient).}
3964	  The decomposition in Sq2 reduces the \(\delta\)-core to a single structural fact, absent
3965	  from Mathlib: the connecting isomorphism
3966	  \[
3967	    \mathtt{pullbackComp}\,\varphi'_{f}\,\varphi'_{h} \;:\;
3968	      \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{f} \,\circ\, \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{h}
3969	      \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,
3970	        \bigl(\varphi'_{f} \;\mathbin{;}\;
3971	          (\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base})^{\mathrm{op}}.\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\,
3972	          \varphi'_{h}\bigr)
3973	  \]
3974	  is a \emph{monoidal} natural isomorphism between the oplax-monoidal functors
3975	  \(\bigl(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{f} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{h},\,
3976	  \mathtt{comp\_}\delta\bigr)\) and \(\bigl(\mathtt{pullback}\,(\varphi'_{f}\mathbin{;}
3977	  \cdots),\,\delta\bigr)\). Writing \(\delta\) for the oplax tensorator of the single
3978	  composite pullback and \(\mathtt{comp\_}\delta = (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{h})
3979	  .\mathrm{map}\,\delta^{\varphi'_{f}} \mathbin{;} \delta^{\varphi'_{h}}\) for the
3980	  composite tensorator of the two-step pullback, monoidality is the identity
3981	  \[
3982	    \delta_{M,N}
3983	      \;=\; (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,\varphi'_{f}\,\varphi'_{h}).\mathrm{inv}_{M\otimes N}
3984	      \;\mathbin{;}\; (\mathtt{comp\_}\delta)_{M,N} \;\mathbin{;}\;
3985	      \bigl((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,\varphi'_{f}\,\varphi'_{h}).\mathrm{hom}_{M}
3986	        \otimes (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,\varphi'_{f}\,\varphi'_{h}).\mathrm{hom}_{N}\bigr).
3987	  \]
3988	  This is precisely the \(\delta\)-twin of the unit coherence
3989	  \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}, and it is proved by the \emph{same} mate-calculus
3990	  argument with the unit comparison \(\eta\) replaced by the tensorator \(\delta\). The
3991	  pivot is that, for the canonical oplax structure on a left adjoint
3992	  (\(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\), here
3993	  \cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}), \(\delta\) is \emph{itself} an adjunction
3994	  transpose: \(\delta_{M,N} = \mathtt{homEquiv}^{-1}\bigl((\eta_{M}\otimes\eta_{N})
3995	  \mathbin{;} \mu_{M,N}\bigr)\), where \(\mu\) is the lax tensorator of the
3996	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\). Consequently the transpose-and-inject opening that closes the
3997	  unit coherence ports verbatim: applying \(\mathtt{homEquiv}.\mathrm{injective}\) for the
3998	  pullback--pushforward adjunction and transporting \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) to
3999	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) across the adjunctions via

1	\chapter{Coherence of locally trivial line bundles}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundleCoherence.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter supplies the cheap, on-path half of the
6	% line-bundle coherence requirement of the A.2.c Quot-scheme embedding
7	% (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}).
8	% The Quot functor takes coherent quotients of a coherent sheaf; to feed it a
9	% line bundle one must know that the line bundle is itself coherent, i.e. a
10	% finitely presented \(\mathcal{O}\)-module. The embedding's input is a point of
11	% the relative Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), whose carrier is the
12	% \emph{locally trivial} predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) of
13	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (the project-side stand-in for an
14	% invertible sheaf, a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules locally isomorphic to
15	% \(\mathcal{O}\) of rank one). The chapter proves
16	% \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial} M \Rightarrow M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\),
17	% together with a rank-one / flatness record. The general implication
18	% ``\(M\) tensor-invertible \(\Rightarrow\) \(M\) locally free of rank one''
19	% (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma~0B8M when stalks are local) is
20	% \emph{not} crossed here: the carrier is already locally trivial, so the
21	% finite-presentation spreading-out is never needed.
22	
23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:lbc_setup}
25	
26	Fix a scheme \(X\) and write \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\) for the category of sheaves
27	of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) on the
28	ringed-space site of \(X\), with structure sheaf \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)).
29	A \emph{line bundle} on \(X\) is an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module; in the
30	sense of \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} we model line bundles by the
31	predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\): a module \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) is locally
32	trivial of rank one if every point \(x \in X\) has an \emph{affine} open
33	neighbourhood \(U\) on which the restriction \(M|_U\) is isomorphic, as an
34	\(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, to the structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U}\):
35	\[
36	  \texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M) \;:=\;
37	  \forall x \in X,\ \exists\, U \ni x \text{ affine open},\quad
38	  M|_U \;\cong\; \struct{U}.
39	\]
40	This is the rank-one case of Stacks Definition~17.25.1
41	(Tag~01CS, invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules), and
42	agrees with the classical ``locally free of rank one'' description by Stacks
43	Lemma~17.25.4 (Tag~0B8M).
44	
45	The goal of the chapter is to record that such a module is \emph{coherent} in the
46	sense the Quot construction consumes, namely finitely presented:
47	\(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) in Mathlib's
48	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) (built from a
49	\(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\)). The mathematics is elementary: on each
50	trivialising chart \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) is finite free of rank one, hence
51	has the trivial finite presentation, and finite presentation is by definition a
52	local condition. No stalk computation and no spreading-out are involved. The
53	content is the bookkeeping that turns the pointwise trivialisation into the
54	indexed \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) Mathlib expects.
55	
56	\section{Site instances for quasi-coherent data}
57	\label{sec:lbc_site_instances}
58	
59	% NOTE (chief unknown). Mathlib's SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation /
60	% QuasicoherentData are stated under three site hypotheses on the slice
61	% topologies J.over X of the underlying site J of X.ringCatSheaf:
62	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat)]
63	%   [∀ x, HasWeakSheafify (J.over x) AddCommGrpCat]
64	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat]
65	% These must be discharged (or assumed as instances) before the statements in
66	% this chapter typecheck for X.ringCatSheaf. The project already lives in the
67	% SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf world, so these should resolve; this is the one
68	% item to verify when the Lean file is scaffolded.
69	
70	The Mathlib predicates \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) and
71	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\) are stated relative to a
72	Grothendieck topology \(J\) on a base category \(C\) and a sheaf of rings
73	\(R : \Sheaf(J, \RingCat)\). They require, for every object \(x : C\), three
74	instances on the slice topology \(J/x\) (Mathlib \(J.\mathtt{over}\ x\)):
75	\[
76	  (J/x).\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}(\forget_{\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat}),\quad
77	  \HasWeakSheafify(J/x, \AddCommGrpCat),\quad
78	  (J/x).\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}(\AddCommGrpCat).
79	\]
80	For \(X.\mathtt{Modules} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\ X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
81	these are the ambient site instances of the ringed space underlying \(X\). We
82	record their availability as a standing assumption for the chapter; verifying it
83	for \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is the single instance-resolution check the
84	formalisation must clear at entry, and is independent of the mathematical
85	content below.
86	
87	\begin{remark}
88	  \label{rem:lbc_site_instances}
89	  For the ringed-space site underlying \(X\) with sheaf of rings
90	  \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), every slice topology \(J/x\) carries the
91	  three instances \(\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}\), \(\HasWeakSheafify\) and
92	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) (for the forgetful functor
93	  \(\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat\)) demanded by
94	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\). Under this assumption the
95	  predicate \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) is well-formed for every
96	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\).
97	\end{remark}
98	
99	\section{From pointwise triviality to a trivialising cover}
100	\label{sec:lbc_cover}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}\leanok
103	[Trivialising affine cover]
104	  \label{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover}
106	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
107	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Definition 17.25.1
108	  % "invertible module" (Tag 01CS), Section 17.25 (Tag 01CR)
109	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4059).
110	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
111	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
112	  %  {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
113	  %  of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
114	  %  the functor
115	  %  $$
116	  %  \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
117	  %  \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
118	  %  $$
119	  %  is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
120	  %  {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
121	  %  to $\mathcal{O}_X$."
122	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CS (Modules, Definition~17.25.1).}
123	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\).
124	  Then there exist an index set \(I\), a family of affine open subschemes
125	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(X\) covering \(X\) (i.e. \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\)), and
126	  for each \(i\) an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
127	  \[
128	    e_i \;:\; M|_{U_i} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \struct{U_i}
129	  \]
130	  to the structure sheaf-as-module on \(U_i\).
131	\end{lemma}
132	
133	\begin{proof}
134	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
135	  \leanok
136	  Apply the hypothesis \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\) at every point \(x \in X\): it
137	  produces an affine open \(U_x \ni x\) and an isomorphism
138	  \(e_x : M|_{U_x} \cong \struct{U_x}\). Take the index set \(I := X\) (the
139	  underlying set of points), and to the index \(x\) assign the chosen
140	  neighbourhood \(U_x\) and the chosen isomorphism \(e_x\). The family
141	  \(\{U_x\}_{x \in X}\) covers \(X\) because each \(x\) lies in its own \(U_x\).
142	  This is exactly the data the definition supplies, packaged as an indexed
143	  family rather than a pointwise existential.
144	\end{proof}
145	
146	\section{The trivial presentation on a chart}
147	\label{sec:lbc_chart_presentation}
148	
149	\begin{lemma}\leanok
150	[Finite free presentation on a chart]
151	  \label{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation}
153	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
154	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
155	  % "Modules of finite presentation" (\label{definition-finite-presentation})
156	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L1377-L1392).
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
158	  % "We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is of {\it finite presentation}
159	  %  if for every point $x \in X$ there exists an open neighbourhood
160	  %  $x\in U \subset X$, and  $n, m \in \mathbf{N}$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
161	  %  is isomorphic to the cokernel of a map
162	  %  $$
163	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{j = 1, \ldots, m}
164	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
165	  %  \longrightarrow
166	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1, \ldots, n}
167	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
168	  %  $$"
169	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Modules of finite
170	  presentation''.}
171	  On each chart \(U_i\) of the trivialising cover of
172	  \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}, the restriction \(M|_{U_i}\) admits the
173	  trivial finite free presentation
174	  \[
175	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \struct{U_i} \;\longrightarrow\; M|_{U_i}
176	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
177	  \]
178	  i.e. \(M|_{U_i}\) is the cokernel of the zero map
179	  \(\bigoplus_{j \in \varnothing} \struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\)
180	  (the case \(m = 0\), \(n = 1\) of the finite-presentation pattern). The
181	  unit sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i}\) (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\))
182	  carries a canonical free presentation on one generator with no relations;
183	  this presentation is finite.
184	\end{lemma}
185	
186	\begin{proof}
187	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
188	  \leanok
189	  The structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,
190	  (U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is free of rank one on the single generator
191	  \(1\): the identity map \(\struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\) exhibits it as
192	  \(\bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), so the surjection
193	  \(\struct{U_i}^{\oplus 1} \twoheadrightarrow \struct{U_i}\) is an isomorphism
194	  and its kernel is zero, generated by the empty family. Thus \(\struct{U_i}\)
195	  has the canonical presentation with \(n = 1\) generators and \(m = 0\)
196	  relations; this canonical \(\mathtt{unit}\)-presentation is the rank-one free
197	  presentation, and Mathlib records it as \emph{finite} (the
198	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\) finite-presentation instance).
199	
200	  The chart presentation is obtained by transporting the canonical finite
201	  presentation of \(\mathtt{unit}\,(U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) along the
202	  isomorphism \(e_i\), using \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso}\).
203	  This construction carries any finite presentation of a module to a finite
204	  presentation of any isomorphic module, so the transported presentation of
205	  \(M|_{U_i}\) is again finite.
206	  % NOTE. The over-restrict categorical bridge is the shared construction
207	  % `Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` (lem:chart_over_iso,
208	  % chap:Picard_SheafOverEquivalence).
209	\end{proof}
210	
211	\section{Finite presentation of a locally trivial line bundle}
212	\label{sec:lbc_finite_presentation}
213	
214	\begin{theorem}\leanok
215	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finitely presented]
216	  \label{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation}
218	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation, rem:lbc_site_instances}
219	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
220	  % Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M), invertible vs locally free of rank one
221	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
223	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
224	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
225	  %  If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
226	  %  the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case)."
227	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~0B8M (Modules, Lemma~17.25.4).}
228	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \cref{rem:lbc_site_instances} and let
229	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\). Then
230	  \(M\) is finitely presented: \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) holds in
231	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\).
232	\end{theorem}
233	
234	\begin{proof}
235	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
236	  \leanok
237	  By \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} choose a trivialising affine cover
238	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) with isomorphisms \(e_i : M|_{U_i} \cong \struct{U_i}\).
239	  Assemble these into a \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) for \(M\): the index type
240	  is \(I\), the covering objects are the affine opens \(U_i\) (which cover \(X\)),
241	  and the presentation attached to index \(i\) is the finite free presentation of
242	  \(M|_{U_i}\) (equivalently, of the restriction \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U_i\))
243	  produced in \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}. Each such presentation is
244	  finite by \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}; the assembled
245	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) therefore satisfies
246	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation}\), and the constructor
247	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk}\) yields
248	  \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\).
249	
250	  The only non-formal input is the trivialising cover; everything else is the
251	  definitional unwinding of finite presentation as a local property
252	  (Stacks, ``Modules of finite presentation''), since a rank-one free module is its own finite
253	  presentation. No stalk computation and no neighbourhood-spreading argument is
254	  used: the hypothesis already provides the local free models.
255	\end{proof}
256	
257	\begin{corollary}\leanok
258	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finite type]
259	  \label{cor:lbc_isFiniteType}
260	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType}
261	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
262	  Under the hypotheses of \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}, the locally
263	  trivial module \(M\) is of finite type (\(M.\mathtt{IsFiniteType}\)). Quasi-coherence
264	  (\(M.\mathtt{IsQuasicoherent}\)) follows from finite presentation via
265	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\).
266	\end{corollary}
267	
268	\begin{proof}
269	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
270	  \leanok
271	  Finite presentation implies finite type by Mathlib's
272	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation}\), immediate
273	  from \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}. Quasi-coherence is immediate from
274	  finite presentation by
275	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\).
276	\end{proof}
277	
278	\section{Rank one and flatness record}
279	\label{sec:lbc_rank_flat}
280	
281	% NOTE. Mathlib (at the pin) has no SheafOfModules-level "locally free of rank r"
282	% or "flat" predicate (verified absent: no SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFree). The
283	% rank-one and flatness facts are therefore recorded chart-locally, as the data
284	% the Quot embedding actually consumes, rather than as instances of a missing
285	% global predicate. Project-side predicates may be introduced if a downstream
286	% consumer needs them packaged; this is flagged for the plan agent.
287	
288	\begin{lemma}\leanok
289	[Chart-local rank one and flatness]
290	  \label{lem:lbc_rank_flat}
291	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one}
292	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
293	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
294	  % "Locally free sheaves" (\label{definition-locally-free}, "(finite)
295	  % locally free of rank $r$")
296	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2079-L2094).
297	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
298	  % "\item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it locally free} if for every
299	  %  point $x \in X$ there exist a set $I$ and an open
300	  %  neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$
301	  %  such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to
302	  %  $\bigoplus_{i \in I} \mathcal{O}_X|_U$ as an $\mathcal{O}_X|_U$-module.
303	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free} if we may
304	  %  choose the index sets $I$ to be finite.
305	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free of rank $r$}
306	  %  if we may choose the index sets $I$ to have cardinality $r$."
307	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Locally free sheaves'' (finite
308	  locally free of rank \(r\)).}
309	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial. The lemma records, at each
310	  point \(x \in X\), a chart \(U \ni x\) together with the trivialising iso
311	  \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) (to \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\)). This iso is the
312	  rank-one free \emph{model}: rank-one freeness and flatness are
313	  \emph{consequences} of this iso, because \(\struct{U}\) is the free
314	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module of rank one and a flat module over itself (a ring is
315	  flat over itself), and both properties transport along \(e\). In the sense of
316	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} this exhibits \(M\) as finite locally free
317	  of rank one and flat on the cover.
318	\end{lemma}
319	
320	\begin{proof}
321	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
322	  \leanok
323	  Immediate from the trivialisation: on \(U_i\), \(e_i\) identifies \(M|_{U_i}\)
324	  with \(\struct{U_i} = \bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), the free module
325	  of rank one. The structure sheaf-as-module is flat over itself, and an
326	  isomorphic module is flat; the index set \(\{*\}\) has cardinality one, so the
327	  rank is one. The properties are recorded chart-locally because rank-one
328	  freeness and flatness are local conditions, and the chart-local data are what
329	  the Quot-scheme embedding consumes when it forms the Hilbert-polynomial input
330	  on a fibre.

5673	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence, lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso, lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
5674	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom,
5675	  % Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM)
5676	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3710--L3721).
5677	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
5678	  % "Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a morphism
5679	  % of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules.
5680	  % If $\mathcal{F}$ is finitely presented and $f$ is flat,
5681	  % then the canonical map
5682	  % $$
5683	  % f^*\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
5684	  % \longrightarrow
5685	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(f^*\mathcal{F}, f^*\mathcal{G})
5686	  % $$
5687	  % is an isomorphism."
5688	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom,
5689	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
5690	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
5691	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
5692	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
5693	  \[
5694	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
5695	  \]
5696	  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
5697	  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
5698	  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)
5699	\end{lemma}
5700	
5701	\begin{proof}
5702	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence, lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso, lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
5703	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
5704	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
5705	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
5706	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
5707	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
5708	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
5709	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
5710	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
5711	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
5712	
5713	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
5714	  definitional equality, and --- crucially --- that the dual is \emph{not sectionwise}.
5715	  Unlike the tensor product, whose value is computed fibrewise
5716	  (\((M \otimes N)(V) = M(V) \otimes N(V)\)), the dual evaluates to a Hom over an entire
5717	  down-set: for an open \(V \subseteq U\),
5718	  \[
5719	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)(V)
5720	      \;=\; \mathcal{H}om\bigl(M|_V,\ \mathcal{O}|_V\bigr)\big|_{\mathtt{Over}\,V},
5721	  \]
5722	  a module of morphisms of presheaves over the whole slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq
5723	  \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\), \emph{not} a single fibre \(M(V) \to \mathcal{O}(V)\). Consequently
5724	  the assertion that restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes with the dual does
5725	  \emph{not} reduce to a single ground-ring reconciliation. The proof proceeds in two
5726	  stages: a preliminary adjunction-uniqueness rewrite (stage~H1) that re-presents the
5727	  pulled-back dual in pushforward form, followed by the resolution of the resulting
5728	  \emph{residual} into \emph{two} legs, carried out objectwise in the open \(V\) (with
5729	  \(V \subseteq U\)) and assembled over the slice with poset-thin naturality.
5730	
5731	  \emph{Stage H1 --- adjunction-uniqueness rewrite.} The pulled-back dual is initially
5732	  presented in the \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)-form inherited from the abstract
5733	  definition of the restriction functor, and no sectionwise comparison can begin against
5734	  that opaque form. We first rewrite it into a \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\)-form: the
5735	  restriction-along-an-open-immersion pullback and the pushforward along the structure-ring
5736	  map \(\beta\) are both left adjoints to one and the same functor, so by uniqueness of left
5737	  adjoints --- the adjunction \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) composed with
5738	  \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) --- they are canonically isomorphic, and this isomorphism
5739	  transports the pulled-back dual into pushforward form. After H1 the residual obligation is
5740	  \emph{exactly}
5741	  \[
5742	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})
5743	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
5744	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val}\bigr),
5745	  \]
5746	  i.e.\ ``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual''. It is this residual ---
5747	  \emph{not} the original Step-4 isomorphism --- that legs (A) and (B) below resolve; legs
5748	  (A) and (B) are the content remaining \emph{after} the H1 rewrite, not a standalone
5749	  two-leg split of the whole step.
5750	
5751	  \emph{Leg (A) --- slice-site Hom base-change (Beck--Chevalley).} The domain presheaf must
5752	  first be transported across the open immersion. Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
5753	  direct-image functor on open sets is fully faithful with image
5754	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); this carries the restricted domain
5755	  \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) to the
5756	  restricted domain \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV) \subseteq
5757	  \mathrm{Opens}\,X\), and hence identifies the two Hom-modules computed over these matched
5758	  slices. This is a genuine slice-site Hom base-change --- a Beck--Chevalley square on the
5759	  internal hom across the fully-faithful open-immersion functor, restricted to the down-set
5760	  of \(fV\) --- \emph{not} a fibrewise identity.
5761	
5762	  \emph{Leg (B) --- ground-ring reconciliation.} The codomain of the internal hom is in
5763	  both cases the structure sheaf. Once leg (A) has matched the domains, the unit codomain
5764	  is reconciled along the open-immersion ring isomorphism
5765	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}_V \colon
5766	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\): restriction of scalars along the
5767	  ring \emph{isomorphism} \(\beta_V\) is an equivalence, and its ModuleCat-level
5768	  shadow on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}, carrying the
5769	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
5770	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly. This is the dual analogue, at the module level, of the
5771	  ring-isomorphism tensor equivalence (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}).
5772	
5773	  % NOTE (iter-260, review — MUST-FIX for plan/writer): the route-(1) sketch below is WRONG.
5774	  % The iter-260 prover proved (DualInverse.lean:229-255) that consuming overEquivalence's
5775	  % restrictOverIso/unitOverIso is STRUCTURALLY INSUFFICIENT for sliceDualTransport: those are
5776	  % sheaf-object isos of restrict↦over / unit↦unit and say NOTHING about dual/internal-hom.
5777	  % The reduced goal's content is that the dual COMMUTES WITH SLICE REINDEXING along
5778	  % f.opensFunctor, which would require overEquivalence's functor (a SheafOfModules.pushforward)
5779	  % to be strong-monoidal CLOSED — the MonoidalClosed structure the project deliberately avoids
5780	  % (rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path). Genuine close = route-(2): build the forward/inverse
5781	  % ≃ₗ directly — leg-A = eqToHom-conjugation across f.opensFunctor (mirroring homLocalSection's
5782	  % naturality, Subsingleton.elim on the thin poset) ∘ leg-B = restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv
5783	  % along (f.appIso V).inv. Plan/writer: rewrite this paragraph to route-(2), and REMOVE the
5784	  % stale \uses edges lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso / lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso
5785	  % (statement+proof blocks, ~lines 5656/5685) — they are NOT consumed by route-(2).
5786	  \emph{The leg-(A) atom \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\).} Leg (A) is realised by a single
5787	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\), which is now a
5788	  \emph{consumer} of the shared open-immersion slice equivalence
5789	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence}\,U\) of
5790	  \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} --- the equivalence of \emph{sheaves of
5791	  modules} over an open immersion (distinct from the fixed-value-category
5792	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}, which operates over a fixed base ring; the
5793	  module-level shared root is the one leg (A) requires), whose functor is the pushforward
5794	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,(\mathtt{phiOver}\,U)\) along the structure-ring comparison of the
5795	  inclusion \(U.\iota\), and whose two coherence legs
5796	  \(\mathtt{restrictOverIso}\) (\cref{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso}) and
5797	  \(\mathtt{unitOverIso}\) (\cref{lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}) supply the comparison of
5798	  the restriction functors and of the unit. Concretely, \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\) is
5799	  the \emph{per-open localization to
5800	  \(V\)} of \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U\): localizing that equivalence at the open
5801	  \(V \subseteq U\) produces exactly the reduced \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear equivalence
5802	  \[
5803	    \bigl(\mathtt{restr}_{fV'}\,M \,\longrightarrow\, \mathtt{restr}_{fV'}\,\mathbf{1}_X\bigr)
5804	      \;\simeq\;
5805	    \bigl(\mathtt{restr}_{V}\,(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta)\,M \,\longrightarrow\,
5806	      \mathtt{restr}_{V}\,\mathbf{1}_Y\bigr)
5807	  \]
5808	  identifying the slice-Hom value over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\) with the slice-Hom value over
5809	  \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\). The close therefore consumes \(\mathtt{restrictOverIso}\) and
5810	  \(\mathtt{unitOverIso}\) localized to \(V\), composed with the canonical bridge from the
5811	  open immersion \(f\) to the inclusion of its image \(U := f.\mathtt{opensRange}\) (the
5812	  isomorphism \(f \cong U.\iota\)) that ports the shared root --- stated for \(U.\iota\) ---
5813	  onto the given \(f\).
5814	
5815	  The reason the localization is light, rather than a heavyweight slice-equivalence
5816	  construction, is the \emph{thinness} of \(\mathrm{Opens}\,X\) and the slices
5817	  \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\): over a thin poset the Hom-set bijection
5818	  \(\mathtt{Functor.FullyFaithful.homEquiv}\) underlying \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U\)
5819	  collapses, at \(V\), to the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation of the slice-Hom components
5820	  along the down-set identity \(\mathtt{image\_preimage\_of\_le}\) --- the image under the
5821	  open immersion of the preimage of an open \(V \subseteq U\) is \(V\) again,
5822	  \(\iota\bigl(\iota^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) --- with naturality discharged by
5823	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\); this is the pattern already supplied axiom-clean for
5824	  \(\mathtt{homLocalSection}\) and \(\mathtt{dualUnitIsoGen}\). The Step-4 residual is then
5825	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.isoMk}\) of the composite (leg (A)
5826	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\)) followed by (leg (B),
5827	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}), with the outer naturality
5828	  thin-poset-trivial. The heavyweight \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \simeq \mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\)
5829	  equivalence object is \emph{not} rebuilt --- it is exactly the localized shared root, and
5830	  in the thin setting its content is the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation above.
5831	  % NOTE: the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module structure on the left-hand Hom of the displayed
5832	  % equivalence is not auto-synthesized; it is supplied via
5833	  % \(\mathtt{Module.compHom}\,(\beta.\mathtt{app}\,V)\), restricting scalars along the
5834	  % open-immersion ring map \(\beta_V\colon \mathcal{O}_X(fV)\to\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\).
5835	
5836	  The residual of stage~H1 (``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual'') is
5837	  the sectionwise composite of leg (A) followed by leg (B), packaged over the slice with
5838	  poset-thin naturality; the full Step-4 isomorphism is then the H1 isomorphism followed by
5839	  this composite. The slice-reindexing coherence is \emph{light}, because \(\mathrm{Opens}\)

5840	  is a thin poset and the relevant squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\),
5841	  whereas H1 and legs (A) and (B) carry the substantive content.
5842	
5843	  It is worth recording \emph{why} the strong-monoidal restriction
5844	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}) has no direct dual analogue here. For
5845	  the tensor product the transport \emph{collapsed to leg (B) alone}: because \(\otimes\)
5846	  is sectionwise, pushing a tensor forward reduced, section by section, to a single
5847	  ground-ring tensor equivalence, with no domain slice left to re-index. The dual's
5848	  non-sectionwise nature removes that collapse and forces the additional leg (A): the
5849	  domain \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) genuinely lives over a varying slice and must be
5850	  transported before the ground ring can be reconciled.
5851	
5852	  We caution that the naive ``fixed-value-category slice equivalence'' of
5853	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} is \emph{not} applicable to leg (A). That instrument
5854	  operates in a fixed value category over a fixed base ring, whereas the residual of
5855	  leg (A) lives at the \emph{presheaf} level, with a per-section ring \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)
5856	  that varies with \(V\) and a finer slicing than the sheaf-level equivalence resolves;
5857	  leg (A) must therefore be built directly at the presheaf-of-modules / varying-ring level.
5858	
5859	  Composing legs (A) and (B) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
5860	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values; assembling these into a
5861	  presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism and sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of
5862	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} resolves the H1 residual. Precomposing with the stage-H1
5863	  rewrite then yields the stated isomorphism
5864	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
5865	
5866	  \emph{The inverse-uniqueness shortcut is not viable.} One might hope to derive the
5867	  isomorphism from the already-established \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} by uniqueness of
5868	  monoidal inverses --- the dual being the \(\otimes\)-inverse of an invertible module and
5869	  restriction commuting with \(\otimes\) --- sidestepping leg (A). The abstract statements of
5870	  this idiom (\(\mathtt{hasRightDualOfEquivalence}\), \(\mathtt{rightDualIso}\)) require,
5871	  however, four structures the present setting lacks: a registered \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\)
5872	  instance on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) (the monoidal structure here is carried entirely
5873	  by hand), a \emph{strong} (not merely lax/oplax) monoidal restriction functor, restriction
5874	  being an \emph{equivalence} (false for a non-surjective open immersion, which is a
5875	  localization), and an exact pairing \(\mathtt{ExactPairing}\,M\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M)\) (which
5876	  exists only for dualizable \(M\), whereas the lemma is stated for general \(M\)). Even
5877	  restricted to the invertible consumer this would require building a coevaluation and
5878	  verifying the zig-zag identities --- strictly \emph{more} infrastructure than leg (A) --- so
5879	  the construction commits to legs (A) and (B) above.
5880	\end{proof}

5882	\begin{lemma}\leanok
5883	[Dual of the structure sheaf]
5884	  \label{lem:dual_unit_iso}
5885	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_unit_iso}
5886	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
5887	  Let \(Y\) be a scheme. The sheaf-level dual of the structure sheaf is canonically the
5888	  structure sheaf itself,
5889	  \[
5890	    \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
5891	  \]
5892	  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf-level ``evaluation at \(1\)'' isomorphism
5893	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\): every endomorphism of the
5894	  unit presheaf is multiplication by its value at the global section \(1\), so evaluation
5895	  at \(1\) identifies the internal hom of the unit into itself with the unit.
5896	\end{lemma}
5897	
5898	\begin{proof}
5899	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
5900	  At the presheaf level the dual of the unit \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_Y\) is the internal
5901	  hom \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1})\), whose value on an open \(U\) is the module
5902	  of \(\mathcal{O}(U)\)-linear endomorphisms of \(\mathbf{1}|_U\). Each such endomorphism is
5903	  determined by its value at the global section \(1\) and equals multiplication by that
5904	  value; hence \emph{evaluation at \(1\)} is itself the sectionwise isomorphism
5905	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\) of presheaves of modules, with
5906	  inverse the scalar-action map \(r \mapsto (\,\cdot\,r)\). (No left unitor is needed: the
5907	  identification is the direct evaluation-at-\(1\) / global-scalar-multiplication
5908	  isomorphism, not the composite through \(\mathbf{1} \otimes \mathbf{1} \cong \mathbf{1}\).)
5909	  Sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} yields
5910	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\).
5911	\end{proof}

5913	\begin{lemma}
5914	
5915	
5916	
5917	\leanok
5918	[The dual of a line bundle is a line bundle]
5919	  \label{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
5920	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_isLocallyTrivial}
5921	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso, lem:dual_unit_iso,
5922	        def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5923	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
5924	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2)
5925	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200--L4213).
5926	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
5927	  % "\item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
5928	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
5929	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
5930	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
5931	  % is an isomorphism."
5932	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR,
5933	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (item~2).}
5934	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
5935	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
5936	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of
5937	  rank one, i.e.\ \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,L)\).
5938	\end{lemma}
5939	
5940	\begin{proof}
5941	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso, lem:dual_unit_iso,
5942	        def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5943	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an affine open \(U\) with a
5944	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\); writing \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) for the
5945	  associated open immersion, it suffices to exhibit an isomorphism
5946	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). This is the following three-step chain:
5947	  \[
5948	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f
5949	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
5950	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U
5951	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_U.
5952	  \]
5953	  \begin{enumerate}
5954	    \item The first isomorphism is the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
5955	      \cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}: restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes
5956	      with the formation of the dual. This is the C-bridge proper; its substantive content
5957	      is the sectionwise ring-iso transport of the module structure
5958	      (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}), as detailed there.
5959	    \item The second isomorphism is the dual applied to the trivialisation
5960	      \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), via the contravariant functoriality of the sheaf-level
5961	      dual in isomorphisms (\(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\),
5962	      \cref{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}): an isomorphism \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\)
5963	      induces \(\mathtt{dual}(L|_U) \cong \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U\).
5964	    \item The third isomorphism trivialises the dual of the unit,
5965	      \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (\cref{lem:dual_unit_iso}):
5966	      the dual of the structure sheaf is the structure sheaf itself, the comparison being
5967	      evaluation at \(1 \in \mathcal{O}_U\), under which
5968	      \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U) \cong \mathcal{O}_U\).
5969	  \end{enumerate}
5970	  Composing the three exhibits \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). Hence
5971	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of rank one, as required by item~2 of Stacks
5972	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (tag 01CR).
5973	\end{proof}

5975	\begin{lemma}
5976	
5977	
5978	\leanok
5979	[Local isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global isomorphism]
5980	  \label{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}
5981	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_restrict}
5982	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi \colon M \to N\) a morphism in
5983	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\).
5984	  Suppose that for every point \(x \in X\) there is an open neighbourhood \(U_x\) of
5985	  \(x\) such that the restriction of \(\varphi\) to \(U_x\) --- the image
5986	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) under the
5987	  restriction functor along the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\colon U_x \to X\) ---
5988	  is an isomorphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_x\). Then \(\varphi\) is an
5989	  isomorphism.
5990	\end{lemma}
5991	
5992	\begin{proof}
5993	
5994	
5995	
5996	  Being an isomorphism for a morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules can be
5997	  checked on the underlying morphism of sheaves of abelian groups, and there it can be
5998	  checked stalkwise. Fix a point \(x \in X\). Choose a preimage \(x' \in U_x\) of \(x\)
5999	  under the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\). By hypothesis the restriction
6000	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) is an
6001	  isomorphism, hence so is its image under any functor --- in particular under the
6002	  stalk-at-\(x'\) functor on the underlying ab-sheaves. The natural isomorphism
6003	  ``restriction commutes with stalks'' (\(\mathtt{restrictStalkNatIso}\)) identifies
6004	  this with the stalk of (the underlying ab-sheaf morphism of) \(\varphi\) at the image
6005	  point \(x\); transporting along it shows that stalk is an isomorphism. As this holds
6006	  at every \(x\), and a morphism of sheaves of abelian groups all of whose stalks are

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application},
10	% \ref{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}) (read from
11	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
16	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
17	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
18	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
19	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
20	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
21	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
22	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
23	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
24	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
25	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
26	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
27	
28	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
29	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
30	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
31	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
32	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
33	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
34	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
35	\[
36	  \begin{array}{ccc}
37	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
38	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
39	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
40	  \end{array}
41	\]
42	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
43	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
44	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
45	
46	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
47	
48	\begin{definition}
49	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
50	  \label{def:cech_nerve}

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE

6	% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and

8	
9	\section{Setup and motivation}
10	
11	This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
12	\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
13	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
14	single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
15	Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
16	\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
17	properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.
18	
19	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
20	{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.
22	
23	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
24	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
25	A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
26	cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
27	\[
28	  \begin{array}{ccc}
29	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
30	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
31	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
32	  \end{array}
33	\]
34	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
35	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
36	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
37

7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).

19	The genus-\(0\) arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
20	(\(J = \Spec k\)); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
21	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\)
22	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
23	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field \(k\), so the
24	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
25	O(-2)) = 0\(, hence \)df = 0\(, hence \)f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
26	characteristic-\(p\) Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant \(f\) can have \(df = 0\). The
27	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
28	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
29	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
30	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
31	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
32	derives the genus-\(0\) base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
33	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
35	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that \(\mathbb P^1\) is
36	\emph{already complete} and \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
37	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.

45

1	\chapter{Auslander--Buchsbaum}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.b sub-row of Route A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the corollary ``regular local ring
8	% \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay'' that A.4.a (the codim-1 extension proof for
9	% Albanese morphisms out of a regular projective surface) consumes. Per
10	% STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
11	% startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the
12	% Albanese geometry and lives entirely under \texttt{RingTheory.*}. The
13	% target Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}
14	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
15	% creating it. Note that Mathlib already houses parts of the depth /
16	% projective-dimension API
17	% (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Depth.*},
18	% \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence},
19	% \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.ProjectiveResolution}); a Plan-Agent audit
20	% at the pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) should confirm what is present
21	% before the prover writes the file, since a fully Mathlib-backed
22	% Auslander--Buchsbaum would collapse this chapter to a re-export.
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma

33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.

1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(k\) in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus \(g = g(C)\).
4	The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of \(C\) in the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety even when \(C(k) = \emptyset\) (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\) and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus \(0\) the witness object is instead the trivial scheme \(\Spec k\), \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement}, the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} via the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (Rigidity Lemma \(+\) additivity Corollary~1.5 \(+\) Corollary~1.2 \(+\) rational-map extension), \emph{cube-free} and \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument. The over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which still carries \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\), is the retained fallback-(a) artifact and is \emph{not} the declaration the genus-\(0\) witness consumes.
5	\section{The Albanese construction}
6	For a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\), the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from \(C\). Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
7	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
8	Let \(C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)\) be the \(g\)\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of \(C\) (the quotient of \(C^g\) by the action of the symmetric group \(S_g\)). It is a smooth proper scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g\). Fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(k)\). The map
9	\[
10	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
11	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
12	\]
13	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing \(P_0\) gives a translation isomorphism \(\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}\), and the Stein factorisation of \(\sigma\) yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
14	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of \(C\) inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
15	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
16	\begin{definition}\leanok
17	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
18	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
19	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
20	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Alb (the Albanese map), FGA Explained ch.~5; arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
21	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every
22	  % such \(b\) arises from a unique map \(a\:B^*\to \IPic_{X/k}\) such that
23	  % \(a(0)=0\).  Since \(B^*\) is integral, \(a\) factors through \(P\).  Thus the
24	  % maps \(a\:B^*\to P\) and \(b\:X\to B\) are in bijective correspondence.
25	  % Plainly, this correspondence is compatible with maps \(b'\:B\to B'\) such
26	  % that \(b'(0)=0\).  In particular, \(1_P\) corresponds to a natural map
27	  % \(u\:X\to A\) such that \(u(x)=0\), and every map \(b\:X\to B\) factors
28	  % uniquely through \(u\)."
29	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Albanese map, rmk:Alb).}
30	  Let \((C, P)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) with a marked \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\). A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) is an \emph{Albanese object} for \((C, P)\) if there exists a morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) sending \(P\) to the identity element \(\eta \in J\), and such that every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\) factors uniquely as \(f = \iota \circ g\) for some morphism of group schemes \(g \colon J \to A\).

1	\chapter{Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology with \(k\)-module coefficients}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_MayerVietoris}
3	
4	This chapter constructs the Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology valued in \(k\)-modules, and develops the \v{C}ech-acyclicity machinery for affine basic-open covers. Together these provide the categorical engine for the Serre finiteness argument (Phase~A step~6). The carriers \(H^n(X,\mathcal F)\) and \(H^{\prime n}(U,\mathcal F)\) live in Chapter~REF and are imported here.
5	
6	The chapter introduces no protected declarations: every definition, lemma, and instance here is auxiliary, supporting the construction of \(H^1(C,\mathcal O_C)\) in Definition~REF.
7	
8	\section{Functoriality of cohomology in the open variable}
9	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
10	
11	The cohomology-of-an-open carrier \(H^{\prime n}(X,\mathcal F)\) is contravariantly functorial in the open variable \(X\). Assembling this functoriality yields a presheaf
12	\[
13	  X \longmapsto H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F),
14	\]
15	and, varying \(\mathcal F\) as well, a bifunctor from sheaves of \(k\)-modules to presheaves of abelian groups. The codomain is the category of abelian groups because \(\Ext\) is always valued there, regardless of the linear enrichment of the source category; the per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is inherited from the reducible wrapper on \(H^{\prime n}\).
16

18	[Cohomology presheaf functor, \(H'\) flavor]

23	  \[
24	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(k, J) \;:\; \Sheaf\,J\,(\ModuleCat\,k) \longrightarrow (C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat)
25	  \]
26	  by the standard composition of the Yoneda embedding, the free-\(k\)-module functor, sheafification, and the \(n\)-th \(\Ext\) functor.
27	\end{definition}

2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.

6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in

15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
